title: Like Father Like Daughter

jokes.text: What's it like having a cauliflower back?
jokes.setup: Louie asks about Tony's boxing match
jokes.punchline: Jokes about cauliflower ears but says 'back' instead
jokes.explanation: Louie's characteristic verbal cruelty with a slight malapropism twist

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: So how'd the boxing match go?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Not so good. I got cauliflower back.

jokes.text: I didn't get creamed. I lost on the decision. I'm impressed. The referee decided I was bleeding too much.
jokes.setup: Tony defends that he didn't get 'creamed'
jokes.punchline: His clarification makes it sound even worse
jokes.explanation: Tony's earnest attempt to save face only makes his loss sound more brutal

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I didn't get creamed. I got beaten up.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: There's a difference.

jokes.text: It's just difficult to figure out what 15% of $4.85 is when somebody's looking at you.
jokes.setup: Customer struggles to calculate tip
jokes.punchline: The pressure of being watched makes simple math impossible
jokes.explanation: Relatable moment of social pressure affecting basic cognitive function

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Let me see... 15% of $32.50...

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: That's... carry the one... no wait...

dialogue.speaker: Taxi Driver
dialogue.line: Take your time, pal.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: I know I'm taking my time! That's the problem! Everyone's staring at me!

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: I can't think when people are watching. My brain just shuts down.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Is it $5? Is it $6? I don't even know anymore!

jokes.text: 73 cents!
jokes.setup: Customer struggling with tip calculation
jokes.punchline: Louie immediately shouts the answer
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - Louie can't help but interrupt with the obvious answer

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You want to know what 15% of $32.50 is? $4.88! And you're gonna give me $5!

jokes.text: No, no pennies, thanks. No pennies.
jokes.setup: Customer finally calculates tip
jokes.punchline: Refuses to take pennies after all that mathematical effort
jokes.explanation: The absurdity of precise calculation followed by rounded rejection

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Refuses to take pennies after all that mathematical effort

jokes.text: I'm up for the part of the horse. Yeah? Which end?
jokes.setup: Bobby reveals he's auditioning for the horse in Equus
jokes.punchline: Tony immediately asks which end
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup and immediate punchline - Tony's directness cuts right to the obvious question

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: I'm auditioning for the horse in Equus.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Which end?

jokes.text: You just put your money in, and it comes right back. You can call anywhere for free.
jokes.setup: Customer discovers broken payphone
jokes.punchline: Excitedly explains the 'feature' to everyone
jokes.explanation: Tourist's innocent excitement about what's clearly a broken phone creates humor

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what? This phone is broken!

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: But look at this — when you pick up the receiver, it automatically hangs up on you!

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: It's perfect! No more long distance charges!

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: You just pick it up, it hangs up. Pick it up, it hangs up.

dialogue.speaker: Customer
dialogue.line: It's the most efficient phone I've ever used!

jokes.text: There's got to be somebody somewhere you want to call. No, well, no, not now.
jokes.setup: Tony encourages Alex to use the free phone
jokes.punchline: Alex's sad admission he has no one to call
jokes.explanation: Darker humor revealing Alex's isolation - more melancholy than laugh-out-loud

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Go ahead, use the phone. It's free.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I have no one to call.

jokes.text: Yeah, it's Bobby Wheeler from New York City calling. No, it's not personal. I'm an actor, too.
jokes.setup: Bobby calls Laurence Olivier's office
jokes.punchline: His earnest introduction as if he's a peer
jokes.explanation: Bobby's delusional confidence in approaching theater royalty as an equal

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Yes, hello, this is Bobby Baccalieri calling for Mr. Olivier. Tell him it's Bobby—we're in the same business.

dialogue.speaker: Secretary
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, who is calling?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Bobby Baccalieri. Just tell him Bobby called. He'll know who I am.

jokes.text: Just tell him that a guy... no... that an actor... named Bobby Wheeler
jokes.setup: Bobby tries to leave a message for Olivier
jokes.punchline: Corrects himself from 'guy' to 'actor' for professional credibility
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment showing Bobby's desperate need for professional validation

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Hi Olivier, it's Bobby. Listen, I ran into this guy— I mean, this actor. This actor who said he knew you.

jokes.text: Just crazy about her. Classiest lady I ever met. Yes, operator, I want the VIP Massage Parlor.
jokes.setup: Tony romanticizes his Bangkok girlfriend as classy
jokes.punchline: Reveals she worked at a massage parlor
jokes.explanation: Classic misdirection - builds up romantic image then undercuts with reality

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: She's a very classy woman. Educated, refined, cultured. She works at this exclusive massage parlor in Bangkok.

dialogue.speaker: Other character
dialogue.line: Tony, a massage parlor?

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: It's very exclusive!

jokes.text: What do you mean busting my chops here making believe you're a regular person?
jokes.setup: Elaine politely introduces herself as new driver
jokes.punchline: Louie's hostile assumption that politeness is fake
jokes.explanation: Pure Louie - suspicious of basic human decency and lashing out accordingly

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Hi, I'm Elaine Nardo. I'm new here.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Yeah? Well, cut the act, sweetheart. Nobody's that nice on their first day.

jokes.text: Fill out this form... And I hope you fill it out better than you fill out your pants.
jokes.setup: Louie gives Elaine paperwork
jokes.punchline: Crude comment about her appearance followed by 'See, we're all fun here'
jokes.explanation: Typical Louie harassment but the punchline lands weakly - more uncomfortable than funny

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: Here's your paperwork.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: You know, you'd be a lot prettier if you smiled more. Your face looks like a catcher's mitt when you frown.

dialogue.speaker: Louie
dialogue.line: See, we're all fun here.

jokes.text: He'd give you the scales off his back.
jokes.setup: Alex tries to defend Louie as generous
jokes.punchline: Says 'scales' instead of 'shirt' implying Louie is reptilian
jokes.explanation: Clever twist on the common saying that perfectly captures how others see Louie

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Louie's generous! He gave me a shirt.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, he gave me scales.

jokes.text: I only work 60 hours a week.
jokes.setup: Alex claims to work part-time like everyone else
jokes.punchline: Reveals his 'part-time' is 60 hours per week
jokes.explanation: Good observation about the self-deception of gig workers - relatably absurd

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I work part-time like everyone else.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Sixty hours a week.

jokes.text: I'm calling from the USA. I'd like to speak to... Number 12.
jokes.setup: Tony calling his Bangkok connection
jokes.punchline: Referring to the woman only by number
jokes.explanation: Shows Tony's romantic delusions about what was clearly a business transaction

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Hello? Yeah, it's Tony Banta from New York... Listen, I need a favor. I'm trying to locate a woman... No, no, I don't have a name. Just refer to her as Number 3.

jokes.text: Since the war's over, they only go up to nine.
jokes.setup: Tony asks for Number 12 at the massage parlor
jokes.punchline: Matter-of-fact explanation about reduced staff post-war
jokes.explanation: Dark but clever - suggests economic realities of post-war sex work with Tony's naive delivery

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I'd like Number 12, please.

dialogue.speaker: Massage Parlor Attendant
dialogue.line: We don't have a Number 12.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Massage Parlor Attendant
dialogue.line: We lost her in the war.

jokes.text: How many times you ever see her? Ten or 20 times. How long were you in Bangkok? A day. Yeah, I think she remembers you.
jokes.setup: Tony wonders if his Bangkok girlfriend remembers him
jokes.punchline: Math reveals 10-20 encounters in one day, Alex's sarcastic response
jokes.explanation: Great mathematical revelation joke - the numbers tell the real story

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: You think she remembers me? We spent one day together in Bangkok.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Tony, if you had 10 to 20 encounters in one day, I'm sure she remembers you.

jokes.text: My ex-husband was right. He's not the worst there is. You're a jerk. A giant jerk.
jokes.setup: Elaine admits Alex isn't the worst
jokes.punchline: Escalates from 'jerk' to 'giant jerk' despite apologizing
jokes.explanation: Shows Elaine's moral clarity - she softens her judgment but doesn't completely let him off

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know, Alex, you're not the worst person in the world.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, thank you, Elaine. That means a lot.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: In fact, you're only a jerk.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Only a jerk?

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: Yes. Most people are giant jerks. You're just a jerk.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're welcome. And I'm sorry for calling you a jerk.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's okay.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You're a giant jerk.

jokes.text: You're not a heel. You're a jerk. A giant jerk.
jokes.setup: Alex explains his situation and Elaine apologizes
jokes.punchline: Elaine doubles down on insulting him instead of apologizing
jokes.explanation: Elaine's moral clarity expressed through escalating insults - shows her backbone

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: I'm sorry, Alex. I shouldn't have said those things.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Thank you, Elaine. I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Elaine
dialogue.line: You know what? Forget it. You're still a jerk.

jokes.text: I feel like we're getting married at Annapolis.
jokes.setup: Everyone encouraging Alex to call his daughter
jokes.punchline: Compares peer pressure to military ceremony formality
jokes.explanation: Unexpected comparison that captures the formal, ceremonial pressure he feels

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, this is just like a military ceremony. Everybody standing around in formation, watching me, waiting for me to do something I don't want to do.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: The only difference is, in the Army, somebody would shoot me if I didn't obey.

jokes.text: Thank you, chambermaid, for your excellent service. I am glad I don't require medical assistance.
jokes.setup: Latka practicing English phrases
jokes.punchline: Bizarrely formal and oddly specific phrases from his book
jokes.explanation: Latka's foreign perspective makes everyday phrases sound absurdly formal

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: The cat is on the mat.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: My hovercraft is full of eels.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: I would like to purchase seventeen umbrellas, but only if they are periwinkle blue and precisely 42 inches in diameter.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: The monkey is wearing a monocle and a fez, and he is dissatisfied with the afternoon's television programming.

jokes.text: Get off my back. Give me a minute while I go to the can.
jokes.setup: Alex suggests better phrases for Latka to learn
jokes.punchline: Practical but crude American expressions vs formal textbook phrases
jokes.explanation: Shows the gap between textbook English and street reality

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, forget those formal phrases. You need to learn real American expressions.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But Alex, my textbook says—

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Forget the textbook. When you're in a cab with someone, you don't say 'I am pleased to make your acquaintance.' You say 'Hey, how's it going?'

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But that is so crude and informal.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: That's America, pal. Crude and informal. You want to fit in, you gotta talk like a real American, not some language record.

jokes.text: What's her major, sardines?
jokes.setup: Learning his daughter is going to college in Portugal
jokes.punchline: Sarcastic guess about Portuguese stereotypes
jokes.explanation: Quick, cynical joke that shows Alex's protective sarcasm about his ex-wife's choices

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Portugal? What am I supposed to do, worry about her getting attacked by wild sardines?

jokes.text: Give me a minute while I go to the can.
jokes.setup: Latka has been practicing Alex's suggested phrases
jokes.punchline: Uses the crude bathroom phrase at exactly the right moment
jokes.explanation: Perfect callback - Latka learned the lesson and applies it contextually, showing character growth

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: Hey, everybody! I've been practicing what Alex taught me. Very useful phrases for American conversation.

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: You know, like when someone is being very stupid or annoying, you say... *pauses for effect* ...go take a crap!

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: And just now, I used it! The dispatcher was being impossible about my shift, so I told him exactly what I learned.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Latka, no! That's not what I meant—

dialogue.speaker: Latka
dialogue.line: But it worked perfectly! He gave me the day off!

jokes.text: I swallowed a whole handful of uppers. You know, when the guy knocked me out, I couldn't close my eyes.
jokes.setup: Tony explains his previous quick drive to Miami using stimulants
jokes.punchline: The drugs kept him conscious even while knocked out
jokes.explanation: Absurd boxing logic - being too stimulated to even go unconscious properly

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: I took some uppers, some downers, and some other pills. The uppers kept me awake for the drive down and the downers kept me mellow. I was doing great until I hit a street sign near Jacksonville.

dialogue.speaker: Tony
dialogue.line: Woke up three hours later in a ditch. But here's the thing - the drugs were so strong, even though I was knocked out, some part of me was still conscious. I could feel myself hitting that sign in my dreams.

jokes.text: Son of a... I beg your pardon?
jokes.setup: Alex sees his daughter for the first time in 15 years
jokes.punchline: Starts to swear in amazement then immediately apologizes
jokes.explanation: Natural father reaction - overwhelmed but immediately trying to be appropriate

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Holy sh— I'm so sorry! I didn't mean to... it's just been so long, and you're... you've grown so much. I apologize, that was completely inappropriate.

jokes.text: Well, I got some bad news for you. I lost the senate race.
jokes.setup: Alex learns his ex-wife told their daughter he's a senator from Montana
jokes.punchline: Plays along with the lie before revealing the truth
jokes.explanation: Good comedic choice to play into the fantasy before deflating it gently

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: A senator from Montana? Why Montana?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, I suppose that's better than the truth. Yeah, okay, I'm a senator from Montana.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Actually, no. I have to tell her the truth. I'm a taxi driver from New York.

jokes.text: Well, I guess she didn't want to impress you.
jokes.setup: Kathy asks why her mother wouldn't tell her about his cab driving
jokes.punchline: Alex's dry observation about the status of his actual job
jokes.explanation: Self-deprecating humor that acknowledges social reality without self-pity

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Well, what's to tell? I'm a cab driver. It's not exactly a secret you keep from your children.

jokes.text: Got your nose. Which is no longer your favorite joke.
jokes.setup: Alex remembers his toddler daughter's favorite joke
jokes.punchline: Acknowledges the obvious that she's outgrown it
jokes.explanation: Touching moment that uses humor to acknowledge the passage of time and lost childhood

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: She used to love this joke. 'Why did the chicken cross the road?' 'To get to the other side!' She'd laugh for five minutes straight.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Now she's eight years old and just stares at me like I'm an idiot.

jokes.text: But it's your driver's license. Oh. Aw, go ahead. That way you can have a picture of me, too.
jokes.setup: Alex gives Kathy his driver's license for his address
jokes.punchline: Realizes it has his photo, decides that's actually a bonus
jokes.explanation: Sweet awkwardness - Alex making the best of an improvised gesture

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Here's my driver's license with my address on it.

dialogue.speaker: Kathy
dialogue.line: Wait, your photo is on here?

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Yeah, so?

dialogue.speaker: Kathy
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? That's a bonus. Now I know exactly who to avoid.

jokes.text: I think I have your smile. Yeah? That's funny. I just got it two seconds ago.
jokes.setup: Kathy says she inherited Alex's smile
jokes.punchline: Alex notes he just started smiling moments ago
jokes.explanation: Beautiful moment - combines genetic inheritance with emotional breakthrough perfectly

dialogue.speaker: Kathy
dialogue.line: I inherited your smile.

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: I just started smiling a few moments ago.

jokes.text: You miss Rona Barrett one morning, you lose touch.
jokes.setup: Bobby's return from failed audition
jokes.punchline: Alex's comment about missing entertainment news
jokes.explanation: Period reference to entertainment reporter - shows Alex's dry humor returning to normal

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: You know, I've been missing the entertainment news. What happened at your audition?

jokes.text: No, uh... they went another way.
jokes.setup: Alex asks if Bobby got the Equus job
jokes.punchline: Bobby's euphemistic way of saying he was rejected
jokes.explanation: Bobby's attempt to maintain dignity in the face of rejection - relatable actor behavior

dialogue.speaker: Alex
dialogue.line: Did you get the Equus job?

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Well, let's just say the horse and I didn't really connect.

jokes.text: Nah, it's cool, you know? No, really... it's cool. It's cool.
jokes.setup: Bobby trying to seem unaffected by rejection
jokes.punchline: Repetitive insistence that it's 'cool' reveals it's definitely not cool
jokes.explanation: Classic over-protestation - the more he says it's fine, the more obvious it is that it isn't

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: It's cool. It's totally cool. I'm cool with it.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: It's fine, it's fine. Everything's fine. I'm fine.

dialogue.speaker: Bobby
dialogue.line: Cool. Cool cool cool. No problem. It's cool.

tmdb_overview: The cabbies volunteer to drive Alex to Miami to meet the daughter he hasn't seen in 15 years.
insight: Pilot establishes ensemble through character comedy, but 71 score suggests uneven landing.
